title: Sticky Keyboard Squeeze
statement_md:
The lab keyboard has gone sticky after years of exam-season chai spills. Whenever someone types a letter it can repeat on its own, so notes files fill up with stretches like `aaaa`.

Your senior hands you a cleanup job: **squeeze** each line by replacing every maximal stretch of the same letter with the letter followed by the length of that stretch.

Write the count **every single time**, even when the stretch is just one letter long. So `aaabb` becomes `a3b2`, and `abc` becomes `a1b1c1`.
input_format_md:
The first line contains a single integer `T` — the number of test cases.

Each of the next `T` lines contains one string `S` made of lowercase English letters — one messy line from the notes file.
output_format_md: For each test case, print the squeezed string on its own line.
constraints_md:
- `1 ≤ T ≤ 10`
- `1 ≤ |S| ≤ 2 × 10^4`
- `S` contains only lowercase English letters `a`–`z`

reference_solution.python:
import sys
from itertools import groupby

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    out = []
    for i in range(1, t + 1):
        s = data[i].decode()
        out.append("".join(ch + str(sum(1 for _ in grp)) for ch, grp in groupby(s)))
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

main()

hints: Walk the string once from left to right, keeping track of how long the current stretch of the same letter has grown. When does a stretch end?

Use two pointers: keep i at the start of a stretch, move j forward while s[j] equals s[i], write the letter plus (j - i), then jump i to j. Runs of length 1 still get their count — and build the answer in a list/StringBuilder, never with repeated string concatenation.
